language.label: Language:
app.title: Study Calculator
guide.title: How to use Study Calculator
guide.intro: Study Calculator helps you solve and learn high-school physics and math. Pick a subject tab at the top, choose a formula or tool, and the program computes the answer while a learning panel on the right explains the theory behind it.
guide.physics.head: Physics
guide.physics.body: Open the Physics tab and pick a section — Mechanics, Thermodynamics, Electromagnetism or Waves. Choose a formula, fill in every field except the one you want to find, and click Compute — the empty field is solved for you.
guide.math.head: Math
guide.math.body: The Math tab holds the symbolic calculator (CAS) for simplifying, factoring, differentiating, integrating and solving expressions, plus a Vectors tab for 2-D and 3-D vector algebra. Type math naturally: ^ is a power and 2x means 2·x.
guide.tools.head: Tools
guide.tools.body: The Tools tab has a unit converter for length, mass, time, speed, energy, pressure, force, temperature and angle. Pick a category, choose the units, and enter a value to convert.
guide.problems.head: Practice problems
guide.problems.body: Inside Physics and Math a Problems tab lists worked questions. Read the statement, then reveal the solution steps and the final answer at your own pace; many problems link to a video walkthrough and the underlying theory.
guide.learning.head: The learning panel
guide.learning.body: The panel on the right of every tab shows key terms, useful formulas, a step-by-step method and a worked example for whatever you are doing. Click a highlighted term or link to open a fuller explanation.
guide.language.head: Language
guide.language.body: Use the Language menu to switch between English, Spanish, French, Russian and Ukrainian at any time — the whole window is re-rendered in the language you pick.
guide.credits.head: Credits & licence
guide.credits.body: Study Calculator was created by Mark Chernyshev, a Peel District School Board student at Applewood Heights Secondary School in Mississauga, Ontario. It is built for Ontario high-school students and their success — free to use, in every classroom and at home. The program is free and open-source software, released under the MIT License: you may use, copy, modify and share it freely, as long as the copyright notice and the licence text are kept with it.

ui.hint: Fill all fields but one — the empty one will be computed.
subject.physics.tagline: Mechanics, thermodynamics, electromagnetism and waves — solve for any variable
subject.math.tagline: Symbolic math · solve, differentiate, integrate — with steps
subject.tools.tagline: Convert between units of length, mass, temperature and more
subject.chemistry.tagline: Solutions, acids & bases, the periodic table and equation balancing
shell.placeholder: This area shows the selected screen — each screen lands with its own redesign issue (#6–#11).
chemistry.coming_soon: Chemistry materials are on their way — formulas, problems and learning notes will appear here soon.
problems.empty: No problems for this subject yet — check back soon.
vector.hint: Type a vector as comma-separated components, e.g. 3, 4 or 1, 2, 3 (2-D or 3-D). The dot product, angle and projection work in either dimension; the cross product gives a vector in 3-D and a scalar in 2-D.
vector.steps_placeholder: Enter the vector(s) and press Compute to see the worked steps.
vector.step.do.subtract: Subtract v from u:  u = {u},  v = {v}.
vector.step.do.scale: Multiply every component of u = {u} by k = {k}.
vector.step.do.dot: Dot product of u = {u} and v = {v}.
vector.step.do.cross: Cross product of u = {u} and v = {v}.
vector.step.cross_3d: In 3-D the result is a vector perpendicular to both u and v.
vector.step.cross_2d: In 2-D the result is the scalar z-component u₁v₂ − u₂v₁.
vector.step.do.unit: Unit vector in the direction of u = {u}.
steps.solve.identify: Identify what you know and the single quantity you want to find — leave that field empty.
steps.solve.isolate: Rearrange the formula to put the unknown on its own (the calculator already does this for every variable).
steps.solve.substitute: Substitute your known values, keeping every quantity in consistent SI units.
steps.solve.compute: Compute the result and check that its size and units make physical sense.
cas.hint: Type an expression (e.g. (x+1)^2 or x^2 - 4). Use ^ for powers; 2x means 2·x; angles are in radians. A constant result also shows a decimal (≈). For "Solve equation" you may write left = right. Leave Variable empty to auto-detect a single unknown.
cas.unavailable:
Symbolic math needs the SymPy library, which is not installed.
Install it with:  pip install sympy
graph.title: Graph
graph.unavailable:
Graphing needs the matplotlib library, which is not installed.
Install it with:  pip install matplotlib
cas.steps_placeholder: Enter an expression and press Compute to see the worked steps.
cas.step.do.simplify: Simplify — cancel common factors and combine like terms.
cas.step.do.expand: Expand — multiply out all products and powers.
cas.step.do.factor: Factor — rewrite the expression as a product of factors.
cas.step.do.integral: Integrate with respect to {var} (indefinite integral; the constant C is omitted).
cas.step.simplify_unchanged: It is already in simplest form, so it stays unchanged.
cas.step.integral_check: Check — differentiating the result gives {back}, the original integrand.
cas.step.do.analyze: Overview — show the main algebraic and calculus views at once.
cas.step.do.series: Expand as a Taylor series around {var} = 0 (up to order {order}).
cas.step.do.logarithm: Apply the laws of logarithms — split products, quotients and powers into a sum of logs.
cas.step.do.identity: Prove the identity by simplifying left − right; it holds only if the difference is 0.
cas.step.identity_true: The difference simplifies to 0 — the identity is TRUE.
cas.step.identity_false: The difference simplifies to {diff} ≠ 0 — it is NOT an identity.
cas.step.do.rate_average: Average rate of change of f over [{a}, {b}] in {var}.
cas.step.do.combine: Combine the two functions: sum, difference, product, quotient and composition.
cas.step.do.function: Analyze the function in {var}: the key features for sketching its graph.
theory.newton_2: Newton's second law: the net force F on a body equals its mass m times its acceleration a (F = m·a). It tells you how strongly a given force accelerates a given mass — double the force and the acceleration doubles; for the same force, double the mass and the acceleration halves.
theory.velocity: For motion with constant acceleration, the final velocity v is the initial velocity v₀ plus the acceleration a multiplied by the elapsed time t (v = v₀ + a·t). Use it whenever the acceleration does not change during the interval.
theory.momentum: Linear momentum p is mass m times velocity v (p = m·v). It measures the 'quantity of motion'; a heavy slow object and a light fast one can carry the same momentum, and total momentum is conserved in collisions.
theory.kinetic_energy: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: E = ½·m·v². It grows with the square of the speed, so doubling the speed quadruples the energy — which is why stopping distances grow so fast with speed.
theory.potential_energy: Gravitational potential energy near Earth's surface is E = m·g·h: the energy stored by lifting a mass m to a height h in a gravitational field g. Letting it fall converts this energy back into kinetic energy.
theory.work: Mechanical work is force times the distance moved along its direction: A = F·s. Work is the transfer of energy by a force; no displacement means no work, however large the force.
theory.power: Power is the rate of doing work: P = A/t, the work A done divided by the time t it takes. The same job done faster requires more power.
theory.heat: The heat needed to change a body's temperature is Q = c·m·ΔT: the specific heat c times the mass m times the temperature change ΔT. Materials with a high specific heat (like water) need a lot of heat for a small temperature rise.
theory.ideal_gas: The ideal gas law links pressure, volume and temperature: P·V = n·R·T, where n is the amount of substance and R the universal gas constant. It describes how a gas responds when you compress, heat or expand it; temperature must be in kelvin.
theory.carnot_efficiency: The Carnot efficiency η = 1 − T_c/T_h is the maximum fraction of heat that any engine working between a cold reservoir T_c and a hot reservoir T_h can turn into work. Real engines always fall below it, and the temperatures must be absolute (kelvin).
theory.linear_expansion: Most solids expand when heated: ΔL = α·L₀·ΔT, where the change in length equals the linear expansion coefficient α times the original length L₀ times the temperature change ΔT.
theory.ohm: Ohm's law: the voltage U across a resistor equals the current I through it times its resistance R (U = I·R). It is the basic relation for simple resistive circuits.
theory.electric_power: The electric power delivered by a circuit element is P = U·I, the voltage across it times the current through it. Combined with Ohm's law this also gives P = I²·R or U²/R.
theory.coulomb: Coulomb's law gives the electrostatic force between two point charges: F = k·q₁·q₂/r². The force grows with each charge and falls off with the square of the distance r — like gravity, but it can attract or repel.
theory.capacitor_charge: A capacitor stores a charge Q proportional to the voltage across it: Q = C·U, where C is the capacitance. A larger capacitance holds more charge at the same voltage.
theory.capacitor_energy: The energy stored in a charged capacitor is W = ½·C·U². Because it grows with the square of the voltage, charging to a higher voltage stores disproportionately more energy.
theory.resistance_series: A wire's resistance is R = ρ·L/S: the resistivity ρ of the material times its length L divided by its cross-sectional area S. Long thin wires resist more; short thick ones resist less.
theory.wave_speed: A wave's speed is its wavelength times its frequency: v = λ·f. For a given medium the speed is fixed, so a higher frequency means a shorter wavelength.
theory.period_frequency: Period and frequency are reciprocals: T = 1/f. The period T is the time for one full cycle; the frequency f is the number of cycles per second.
theory.photon_energy: A photon's energy is proportional to its frequency: E = h·f, where h is Planck's constant. Higher-frequency light (toward the blue and ultraviolet) carries more energy per photon.
theory.wavelength_light: For light in vacuum, wavelength and frequency are tied together by the speed of light: λ = c/f. A higher frequency means a shorter wavelength, since c is constant.
theory.snell: Snell's law describes refraction at a boundary: n₁·sin θ₁ = n₂·sin θ₂. Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (higher refractive index n) and away from it when leaving.
error.no_real_solution: No real-valued solution (square root of a negative value).
error.not_finite: The result is out of range (too large or undefined).
error.total_internal_reflection: Total internal reflection — the angle does not exist.
error.no_empty_field: Leave exactly one field empty — it will be computed.
error.too_many_empty: Several fields are empty ({fields}). Leave exactly one.
error.unknown_unit: Unit does not belong to this category.
error.cas_needs_variable: This operation needs a variable (e.g. x).
error.cas_bad_variable: "{value}" is not a valid variable name.
error.cas_ambiguous_variable: Several unknowns ({vars}). Type which one to use in the Variable field.
error.cas_not_inequality: Type an inequality using <, <=, > or >= (e.g. x^2 - 4 > 0).
error.cas_needs_point: Enter the point to use (a number, or oo for infinity).
error.vec_parse: Could not read the vector "{value}". Use 1, 2, or 3 numbers separated by commas.
error.vec_dim_mismatch: The vectors must have the same dimension (got {a} and {b}).
error.vec_cross_dim: The cross product needs 2-D or 3-D vectors.
error.vec_zero: This is undefined for a zero vector.
error.vec_bad_scalar: "{value}" is not a valid number for the scalar k.
error.chem_parse: Could not read "{text}" as a chemical formula.
error.chem_no_arrow: Separate reactants and products with an arrow (->).
theory.molarity: Molar concentration (molarity) measures how much solute is dissolved: c = n / V, where n is the amount of solute in moles and V the solution volume in litres. The unit is mol/L (M). Rearrange to find any quantity from the other two.
theory.dilution: When a solution is diluted the amount of solute is unchanged, so c₁V₁ = c₂V₂. Knowing any three of the initial and final concentrations and volumes gives the fourth — the basis of preparing a dilute solution from a concentrated stock.
theory.moles: The amount of substance n links mass m and molar mass M by n = m / M. The molar mass (g/mol) is the sum of the standard atomic weights in the formula — compute it with the Molar mass box on the Periodic table tab.
theory.ph: The pH measures acidity: pH = −log₁₀[H⁺], where [H⁺] is the hydrogen-ion concentration in mol/L. A lower pH is more acidic; at 25 °C pure water has pH 7. Invert with [H⁺] = 10^(−pH).
